paragraphs.text: To understand the simplest bond, one must first understand the simplest atom. Hydrogen gives us the cleanest possible beginning, because there is only one proton and one electron. The whole quantum problem therefore reduces to describing how that electron is distributed in space. The wave function \( \psi \) is the underlying amplitude, while \( \psi^2 \), more precisely \( |\psi|^2 \), gives the probability density. The normalization condition says that the total probability over all space is one, because the electron must be found somewhere.

For the ground state, the only orbital that matters here is the \(1s\) orbital. It is spherically symmetric about the nucleus, and its density is concentrated most strongly near the center before falling off rapidly with distance. This rapid fall is already physically suggestive. About ninety percent of the electron density lies within a sphere of radius \(1.4\,\text{\AA}\), and better than ninety nine percent lies within \(2\,\text{\AA}\). The orbital therefore has no hard edge, yet it is nevertheless strongly localized. Energetically, this state lies \(13.60\ \text{eV}\) below a completely separated proton and electron.

The chapter is right to insist on pictures. A single contour, the usual classroom circle, is useful because it gives a quick visual boundary for where the electron is likely to be found. But it also conceals something important, namely that the density does not stop abruptly. Nested contours show the gradual fading more honestly, and the cloud picture says the same thing in statistical language by imagining many repeated detections of the electron. That is the intuition behind the chemist's phrase electron density. Figure 1.1 is therefore not ornamental. It is teaching three different ways of looking at the same distribution.

visuals.label: Figure 1.1
visuals.caption: Three complementary pictures of the hydrogen 1s orbital: a single contour, a richer contour family, and a cloud picture. The learner should notice that each picture expresses the same probability distribution in a different visual language.

paragraphs.text: Because the orbital is spherically symmetric, one can ask sharper radial questions. One can ask what fraction of the cloud lies outside radius \(r\), and one can ask for the probability of finding the electron between \(r\) and \(r+dr\). The second question produces the radial density, and here the picture becomes more subtle. The radial density peaks at \(0.529\,\text{\AA}\), the Bohr radius \(a_0\). So although the wave function itself is largest at the nucleus, the probability of finding the electron exactly there is very small. Figure 1.2 also reminds us not to confuse model-based radii with empirical ones. The van der Waals radius is a structural average extracted from molecular measurements, not a fundamental radius built into the one-electron hydrogen problem. With that distinction in place, the stage is set for the first molecule.

visuals.label: Figure 1.2
visuals.caption: Two radial plots for the hydrogen 1s state. One shows how much charge lies outside a sphere of radius r, and the other shows where the radial probability density is greatest, namely near the Bohr radius.

paragraphs.text:
When two hydrogen atoms are brought within bonding distance, the problem changes in character. We are no longer describing one electron around one center. We need a wave function spread over two nuclei. The first approximation is the standard LCAO move: we assume that the molecular orbital can be built from the two familiar \(1s\) atomic orbitals of the separated atoms. Thus Equation 1.1 writes
\[
\psi = c_1\phi_1 + c_2\phi_2.
\]
Here \( \phi_1 \) and \( \phi_2 \) are the two hydrogen \(1s\) functions, and the coefficients \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) measure how much each one contributes to the molecular orbital.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 1 writes the molecular orbital as a linear combination of the two hydrogen one s orbitals.
equations.note: This is the basic LCAO ansatz. The coefficients measure how much each atomic orbital contributes to the molecular orbital.

paragraphs.text:
The density comes from squaring that combination. Equation 1.2 is therefore not a mere algebra exercise. It is the place where bonding first appears mathematically:
\[
\psi^2 = \left(c_1\phi_1 + c_2\phi_2\right)^2
       = (c_1\phi_1)^2 + (c_2\phi_2)^2 + 2c_1c_2\phi_1\phi_2.
\]
The first two terms are just the separate atomic densities. The third term is the decisive one. If \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) have the same sign, then the cross term adds density in the region where the orbitals overlap. If the coefficients have opposite signs, then the cross term subtracts density there. The difference between bonding and antibonding is already present in that one interference term.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 2 shows that squaring the trial orbital produces two atomic density terms and one interference term.
equations.note: The cross term is the decisive part. Its sign determines whether density is built up or depleted in the overlap region.

paragraphs.text: That is why two qualitatively different combinations emerge. When the two orbitals are in phase, electron density is enhanced between the nuclei, and the negative charge in that internuclear region helps hold the two positive nuclei together. When the two orbitals are out of phase, a nodal plane appears between the nuclei, the wave function changes sign across it, and the electron density in the very region needed for binding is depleted. Figure 1.3 makes this contrast visible. The lower orbital is bonding because it draws density into the middle. The upper orbital is antibonding because it creates a zero there.

visuals.label: Figure 1.3
visuals.caption: The bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals obtained by combining two hydrogen 1s orbitals. The crucial contrast is whether electron density is built up or removed between the nuclei, which in turn lowers or raises the energy.

paragraphs.text:
To measure overlap quantitatively, the chapter introduces Equation 1.3,
\[
S_{12} = \int \phi_1\phi_2\,d\tau.
\]
This overlap integral does exactly what its name suggests: it measures how much the two basis orbitals inhabit the same region of space. If the atoms are very far apart, \(S_{12}\) is essentially zero. As the atoms move together, the value grows. Figure 1.4 therefore has a clear conceptual role. It shows that the interaction between the atoms is not an on off switch. It strengthens continuously as the orbitals interpenetrate.

visuals.label: Figure 1.4
visuals.caption: The overlap integral S for two hydrogen 1s orbitals as a function of internuclear distance. The main lesson is that interaction grows continuously as the orbitals interpenetrate.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 3 defines the overlap integral, which measures how much the two orbitals occupy the same region of space.
equations.note: Greater overlap means stronger coupling between the two centers.

paragraphs.text:
We can now derive the approximate orbital energies that the chapter later quotes as Equations 1.4 and 1.5. The starting point is the one-electron Schrödinger equation
\[
H\psi = E\psi,
\]
together with the LCAO ansatz
\[
\psi = c_1\phi_1 + c_2\phi_2.
\]
The strategy is not to solve the two-center problem exactly. Instead, we assume from the outset that the molecular orbital lives in the two-dimensional space spanned by \( \phi_1 \) and \( \phi_2 \), and then ask which values of \(E\) make that approximation self-consistent.

Substitute \( \psi = c_1\phi_1 + c_2\phi_2 \) into \(H\psi=E\psi\). Then multiply first by \( \phi_1 \) and integrate over all space, and then multiply by \( \phi_2 \) and integrate again. This gives
\[
c_1\langle \phi_1|H|\phi_1\rangle + c_2\langle \phi_1|H|\phi_2\rangle
=
E\big(c_1\langle \phi_1|\phi_1\rangle + c_2\langle \phi_1|\phi_2\rangle\big),
\]
\[
c_1\langle \phi_2|H|\phi_1\rangle + c_2\langle \phi_2|H|\phi_2\rangle
=
E\big(c_1\langle \phi_2|\phi_1\rangle + c_2\langle \phi_2|\phi_2\rangle\big).
\]
For a homonuclear diatomic molecule, symmetry gives
\[
\langle \phi_1|H|\phi_1\rangle = \langle \phi_2|H|\phi_2\rangle = \alpha,
\]
\[
\langle \phi_1|H|\phi_2\rangle = \langle \phi_2|H|\phi_1\rangle = \beta,
\]
and
\[
\langle \phi_1|\phi_2\rangle = \langle \phi_2|\phi_1\rangle = S,
\qquad
\langle \phi_1|\phi_1\rangle = \langle \phi_2|\phi_2\rangle = 1.
\]
So the two equations reduce to
\[
(\alpha-E)c_1 + (\beta-ES)c_2 = 0,
\]
\[
(\beta-ES)c_1 + (\alpha-E)c_2 = 0.
\]

A nontrivial molecular orbital requires \(c_1\) and \(c_2\) not both to vanish, so the determinant of the coefficient matrix must be zero:
\[
\begin{vmatrix}
\alpha-E & \beta-ES\\
\beta-ES & \alpha-E
\end{vmatrix}=0.
\]
Hence
\[
(\alpha-E)^2 - (\beta-ES)^2 = 0,
\]
so
\[
\alpha-E = \pm(\beta-ES).
\]
At this stage the two possible signs correspond to the two possible molecular orbitals. The algebra has now compressed the whole splitting problem into one clean bifurcation.

Take first the plus sign:
\[
\alpha-E = \beta-ES.
\]
Rearranging gives
\[
E(1-S)=\alpha-\beta,
\]
and therefore
\[
E_{\text{antibonding}} = \frac{\alpha-\beta}{1-S}.
\]
Now take the minus sign:
\[
\alpha-E = -(\beta-ES),
\]
which gives
\[
E(1+S)=\alpha+\beta,
\]
and therefore
\[
E_{\text{bonding}} = \frac{\alpha+\beta}{1+S}.
\]
These are Equations 1.4 and 1.5. One can now see exactly where their structure comes from. The numerators come from Hamiltonian matrix elements, while the denominators come from overlap, that is, from the fact that the two basis orbitals are not orthogonal.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 4 gives the bonding orbital energy.
equations.note: The bonding root comes from the in phase combination. The denominator shows that overlap affects normalization as well as coupling.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 5 gives the antibonding orbital energy.
equations.note: The antibonding root comes from the out of phase combination. Because the denominator is smaller, the antibonding level is pushed upward more strongly.

paragraphs.text:
The symbols \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) now deserve a proper interpretation. The Coulomb integral \( \alpha \) sets the energy scale of the electron when it is associated with one center:
\[
\alpha = \int \phi_1 H \phi_1\,d\tau.
\]
The resonance integral \( \beta \) encodes the energetic effect of coupling the two centers:
\[
\beta = \int \phi_1 H \phi_2\,d\tau.
\]
The chapter is wise to warn against the word resonance here. Nothing is literally oscillating back and forth. Delocalization is the better intuition. In this two-center hydrogen problem, \( \beta \) is negative, and that one sign lowers the bonding energy while raising the antibonding energy.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 6 defines the Coulomb integral alpha.
equations.note: Alpha measures the one center energy scale for the electron.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 7 defines the resonance integral beta.
equations.note: Beta measures the energetic effect of coupling the two centers. In this hydrogen problem it is negative, which stabilizes the bonding combination and destabilizes the antibonding one.

paragraphs.text: These one-electron orbital energies are not yet the whole molecular energy. Figure 1.5 shows why. As the two atoms approach, the electronic energy associated with a filled bonding orbital falls, but the positively charged nuclei repel one another more and more strongly. The observed bond length comes from the competition between these two tendencies. Their sum gives the familiar Morse-like well with a minimum near the equilibrium distance. If instead the antibonding orbital were occupied, the electronic term would fail to provide enough shielding between the nuclei, and the total energy would rise rather than settle into a minimum.

visuals.label: Figure 1.5
visuals.caption: Electronic attraction, nuclear repulsion, and total energy as functions of the H-H distance. A stable bond appears only when electronic stabilization can more than offset nuclear repulsion over some range of distances.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 4 supplies the electronic contribution for the bonding case.
equations.note: As the atoms approach, the bonding electronic term falls and competes with nuclear repulsion.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 5 supplies the electronic contribution for the antibonding case.
equations.note: With antibonding occupation the electronic term does not create a stable minimum against nuclear repulsion.

paragraphs.text: The same formulas already explain an asymmetry that the pictures alone might not make fully clear. Because the denominators are \(1+S\) and \(1-S\), the antibonding denominator is smaller than the bonding one whenever \(S>0\). That means the antibonding level is pushed upward more strongly than the bonding level is pulled downward. This imbalance matters immediately for helium. Two helium atoms would place two electrons in the bonding orbital and two in the antibonding one. Since the antibonding penalty outweighs the bonding gain, the net result is not a stable \(\mathrm{He}_2\) molecule in this simple scheme.

Even for \(\mathrm{H}_2\), however, placing two electrons in the bonding orbital does not give exactly twice the one-electron stabilization. The reason is familiar and profound. Two electrons with opposite spin may occupy the same orbital, but they still repel one another because they still live in the same general region of space. The simplest treatment averages that repulsion. A more refined treatment allows the electrons to avoid one another dynamically. That extra adjustment is what the chapter calls electron correlation, and when it is incorporated through a more elaborate combination of configurations, one speaks of configuration interaction.

The contour plots on page 6 make the qualitative geometry more vivid. Figure 1.6 shows that in the bonding orbital the density is pulled inward toward the midpoint between the nuclei, while farther out the contours form a smooth oval envelope around the two atoms. In the antibonding orbital the opposite trend appears. The nodal plane remains at the center, but the outer density is pushed toward the far sides of the nuclei. One can almost read the force picture directly from the contours. The bonding orbital gathers charge where it can bind. The antibonding orbital redistributes charge in a way that favors separation.

visuals.label: Figure 1.6
visuals.caption: Contour plots of the bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals of H₂. The main point is the contrast between inward drawn density in the bonding case and outward displaced density in the antibonding case.

visuals.label: Figure 1.7
visuals.caption: Plots of psi squared for the H₂ molecular orbitals along the internuclear axis, compared with the isolated atomic densities. They show directly how bonding increases density in the middle, whereas antibonding removes it.

paragraphs.text: Figure 1.7 removes the distraction of sign by plotting \( \psi^2 \) along the internuclear axis. The solid curves represent the molecular orbitals, while the dashed curves represent the undisturbed atomic densities. In the bonding case, the density is slightly contracted and enhanced in the middle relative to the simple sum of the isolated atomic densities. In the antibonding case, density is removed from the middle and displaced outward. So the word bonding is not a metaphor imposed from outside. It describes a literal redistribution of probability into the region where the nuclei can be held together.

Finally, the coefficient pattern itself can be estimated. For a homonuclear diatomic molecule, symmetry requires equal magnitudes on the two atoms. If one momentarily ignores the small overlap correction in the normalization, then the bonding orbital must satisfy
\[
c_1^2 + c_2^2 = 1,
\qquad
|c_1| = |c_2|,
\]
so
\[
c_1 = c_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707.
\]
The antibonding orbital has the same magnitudes but opposite signs:
\[
c_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},
\qquad
c_2 = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.
\]
That is the clean introductory picture. Strictly speaking, the exact normalization should include the overlap \(S\), which makes the antibonding coefficients slightly larger in magnitude than the bonding ones. Even this small correction is conceptually useful, because it is yet another way of seeing why antibonding is somewhat more forceful than bonding in the corresponding pair of orbitals.

When three hydrogen atoms are brought together, the neat two by two story of H two is no longer enough. We now have three atomic orbitals to combine, so there is no single interaction diagram of the old sort that captures everything at once. The chapter therefore chooses a clever intermediate step. First form an H two unit from two atoms, and then let the third hydrogen one s orbital interact with the bonding and antibonding orbitals of that pair. Figure 1.8 shows this construction.

visuals.label: Figure 1.8
visuals.caption: A symmetry based construction of triangular H3 from an H2 fragment and a third hydrogen one s orbital. The figure shows which orbitals are allowed to interact and which one is left untouched.

paragraphs.text: At this point symmetry becomes a governing rule rather than a decorative label. Two orbitals can interact only if they have the same symmetry with respect to every symmetry element that matters. The reason is simple. If one orbital is symmetric and the other is antisymmetric under a reflection, then their product changes sign under that reflection, so the interaction integral cancels to zero when integrated over the whole space. In triangular H three, the important nontrivial element is the y z plane. The sigma orbital of H two and the one s orbital of the third atom are symmetric with respect to that plane, whereas sigma star is antisymmetric. So the symmetric pair may mix, producing sigma one and sigma two star, while sigma star remains unchanged. Sigma one is lower because every lobe cooperates in the same sign pattern. Sigma two star is higher because a node now separates the top hydrogen from the lower pair, and the net pattern contains more antibonding than bonding.

visuals.label: Figure 1.8
visuals.caption: The orbitals of triangular H3 labeled by symmetry with respect to the y z plane. The important point is that only the symmetric pair mixes, giving sigma one and sigma two star.

paragraphs.text: Once the orbitals are built, the electrons are placed into them. This is the aufbau idea in one of its simplest forms. H three plus, with two electrons, puts both into sigma one and is electronically stabilized relative to a proton plus H two. Neutral H three, with three electrons, still remains overall bonding, because the third electron enters sigma two star only once. But H three minus, with four electrons, fills sigma one and sigma two star, and the antibonding cost wins. Figure 1.9 then compares the triangular and linear arrangements. The same symmetry logic survives in the linear case, but the lower pair of hydrogens no longer overlap with each other, so both the bonding and antibonding splittings become smaller.

visuals.label: Figure 1.9
visuals.caption: A comparison of the orbital energies for triangular and linear H3. The same symmetry pattern survives, but the splittings shrink when direct overlap between the lower hydrogens is removed.

sections.title: Four Hydrogen Atoms and Molecular Repulsion

paragraphs.text: The H four problem is the next clear lesson in how molecules begin to repel other molecules. The chapter chooses a tetrahedral arrangement and builds it from two H two fragments placed at right angles. One lies along the x axis and the other along the y axis. With respect to the x z and y z planes, the two bonding orbitals are both symmetric on both planes, so they are labeled S S. The two antibonding orbitals carry mixed symmetry, one S A and the other A S. That immediately tells us which orbitals can talk to each other. Only the two S S orbitals have matching symmetry, so only they mix to produce a lower sigma one and a higher sigma two star.

visuals.label: Figure 1.10
visuals.caption: The orbitals of tetrahedral H4 arranged by their symmetry with respect to the x z and y z planes. The figure makes clear that only the two S S bonding orbitals can interact.

paragraphs.text:
The coefficient pattern now changes in a revealing way. In the simple H two case, equal sharing between two centers gave coefficients of one over root two in magnitude. Here the bonding combination is spread over four equivalent hydrogen one s contributions, so normalization gives
\[
4c^2 = 1, \qquad c = \frac{1}{2}.
\]
That is why the chapter says the coefficients are zero point five rather than zero point seven zero seven. This also explains the otherwise puzzling comparison between sigma two star and the unchanged antibonding orbitals. Sigma two star contains more antibonding contacts in count, but each contact is weighted by smaller coefficients. Overlap effects scale with coefficient products and with the overlap integral of Equation 1.3. So two interactions built from one half times one half can amount to roughly the same as one interaction built from one over root two times one over root two.

visuals.label: Figure 1.10
visuals.caption: The tetrahedral H4 construction used to compare bonding and antibonding combinations with different coefficient sizes. It is the right picture for seeing why the new antibonding orbital is not driven higher than the unchanged ones.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 3 defines the overlap integral as the integral of phi one times phi two over all space.
equations.note: The key idea is that stronger spatial overlap supports stronger interaction between the orbitals.

paragraphs.text: Now fill the orbitals with four electrons, one pair from each H two fragment. Two electrons occupy sigma one and two occupy sigma two star, while the unchanged antibonding orbitals remain empty. The net result is repulsion. So two H two molecules do not merge into a stable H four molecule. This is more than a curiosity. It is the first stripped down explanation of why ordinary molecules usually keep their identity when they approach each other. Closed shell fragments typically repel because filled orbitals interacting with filled orbitals produce as much or more antibonding than bonding. A chemical reaction must first overcome that repulsion, and that is one major piece of what we later call activation energy.

visuals.label: Figure 1.10
visuals.caption: The same H4 orbital scheme now read as a lesson in intermolecular repulsion. Filling both the lower and higher S S combinations removes any net bonding advantage.

sections.title: Carbon Atomic Orbitals and Directional Overlap

paragraphs.text: With carbon the story becomes richer because the valence shell contains both s and p orbitals. The carbon one s orbital can be ignored for bonding, not because it ceases to exist, but because it is too deep and compact to overlap significantly with other valence orbitals. It behaves as a core shell that mainly shields the nucleus. The chemically relevant orbitals are therefore the two s and the three two p orbitals. The two s orbital is still spherically symmetric, but now it contains a spherical node. Outside that inner nodal structure it behaves much like a larger and more weakly bound version of a one s orbital. Figure 1.11 shows the wave function, the contour picture, and the conventional simplified drawing. Its energy is about minus nineteen point five electron volts, noticeably lower than the hydrogen one s orbital because the carbon nucleus pulls more strongly.

visuals.label: Figure 1.11
visuals.caption: Three views of the carbon two s orbital: wave function, contours, and conventional sketch. The important feature is the inner spherical node together with an outer shape that still resembles an s orbital.

paragraphs.text: The three two p orbitals differ from two s not merely in energy but in geometry. Each has one nodal plane and two lobes of opposite sign, and the three point along the x, y, and z axes. Figure 1.12 makes this visible first as a one dimensional wave function, then as contours, and finally as the conventional narrow lobe drawing used in chemistry. The two p set lies higher, at about minus ten point seven electron volts, because these orbitals keep the electrons on average farther from the nucleus. Yet if one squares all three p orbitals and adds the densities, the directional preferences wash out and one recovers an overall spherical distribution. So the anisotropy lies in the individual orbitals, not in the fully averaged p shell.

visuals.label: Figure 1.12
visuals.caption: Three complementary views of a carbon two p x orbital. The figure shows the nodal structure, the two opposite lobes, and the simplified drawing chemists use in ordinary orbital diagrams.

paragraphs.text:
The new ingredient is directional overlap. A hydrogen one s orbital can overlap head on with a carbon two p orbital, and two carbon p orbitals can also overlap head on. Figure 1.13 plots both cases. In the one s with two p case, the overlap first grows, then reaches a maximum, and finally falls to zero when the hydrogen orbital sits over the carbon center. That last cancellation is crucial. The front lobe of the p orbital helps, but the back lobe hurts by exactly the same amount. In the p with p case, the same sign front lobes initially cooperate, but as the nuclei move too close, the front lobes and back lobes begin to cancel and the overlap falls again. When the approach is not perfectly head on, the overlap is reduced because less density is built between the nuclei. In fact the one s to two p overlap follows the simple angular rule
\[
S_{1s,2p} \propto \cos\theta,
\]
so the overlap is greatest at theta equals zero and vanishes when the hydrogen lies in the nodal plane at theta equals ninety degrees.

visuals.label: Figure 1.13
visuals.caption: Overlap curves for head on s with p and p with p interactions on carbon. The crucial lesson is that overlap grows, reaches a maximum, and then can cancel again because opposite sign lobes begin to interfere.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 3 defines the overlap integral as the integral of phi one times phi two over all space.
equations.note: The key idea is that stronger spatial overlap supports stronger interaction between the orbitals.

paragraphs.text: Methane combines everything developed so far into the first truly familiar carbon molecule. There are eight valence electrons to place, four from carbon and one from each hydrogen. The chapter begins from the H four unit of Figure 1.10 and then lets its symmetry adapted orbitals interact with the carbon valence orbitals. A tetrahedral arrangement is chosen because it keeps the hydrogens as far apart as possible while still allowing all four of them to bond to carbon. With respect to the x z and y z planes, carbon two s is S S, carbon two p x is S A, carbon two p y is A S, and carbon two p z is S S. So the symmetry bookkeeping already tells us which pairs of orbitals may mix.

visuals.label: Figure 1.14
visuals.caption: The molecular orbitals of methane built from tetrahedral H4 and the carbon valence orbitals. The diagram is a symmetry matching exercise that shows why the tetrahedral arrangement is so effective.

paragraphs.text: This immediately explains the overall pattern in Figure 1.14. The carbon two s orbital can mix with the lower S S hydrogen combination, and carbon two p z can mix with the other S S combination. Carbon two p x mixes with the S A hydrogen orbital, and carbon two p y mixes with the A S one. That gives four bonding orbitals and, higher up, four antibonding partners. One bonding orbital lies lower than the other three because it contains more two s character, and two s starts from a lower atomic energy than the p orbitals. The interesting exception is the interaction between carbon two s and sigma two star of the hydrogen framework. Its overlap is zero. The lower lobes contribute bonding and the upper lobes contribute equal antibonding, so the integral cancels and nothing comes of that pairing.

visuals.label: Figure 1.14
visuals.caption: The methane orbital scheme with four bonding interactions and one symmetry forbidden pairing. The cancellation between carbon two s and sigma two star is the main subtlety to notice.

paragraphs.text: One of the most important lessons is that a C H bond is not tied to a single molecular orbital in this delocalized picture. Each hydrogen is held to carbon by contributions from several occupied orbitals at once. The two lower hydrogens in Figure 1.14 receive bonding from the low all s combination and also from the orbitals involving carbon two p z and two p x. The upper hydrogens receive bonding from the low all s combination and from the orbitals involving carbon two p y and two p z. So the ordinary structural formula with four equivalent bonds is useful, but it hides the true molecular orbital bookkeeping. The electron distribution is spread across a coordinated set of orbitals rather than parcelled out into four isolated sticks.

visuals.label: Figure 1.14
visuals.caption: A bookkeeping diagram showing that each C H bond in methane receives support from several occupied molecular orbitals rather than from a single isolated bond orbital.

paragraphs.text: That is why Figure 1.15 matters. The conventional drawings in Figure 1.14 are excellent for seeing which basis orbitals contribute, but they are poor pictures of the actual electron distribution. The more realistic wire mesh contours show fat, fused shapes rather than neat little lobes touching at a point. One can still recognize the underlying s and p ingredients, but now the resulting molecular orbitals look like genuine three dimensional charge clouds. Methane therefore closes this batch with an important methodological lesson. Simple orbital cartoons are useful analytic tools, but one should always carry in the background a fuller picture of the real spatial density they are trying to summarize.

visuals.label: Figure 1.15
visuals.caption: Wire mesh contours for the four filled molecular orbitals of methane. These shapes correct the oversimplified cartoon picture by showing fuller three dimensional electron density.

sections.title: Methylene and the energetic preference for bending

paragraphs.text:
Methylene, \( \mathrm{CH_2} \), is simpler than methane in atom count but subtler in orbital logic, because one symmetry class now contains more than two orbitals that can mix.
Figure 1.16 makes the issue visible: the bonding combination from the two hydrogens, together with the carbon \(2s\) and \(2p_z\) orbitals, all have the same overall symmetry and therefore belong to one coupled block.
At the same time, the antibonding hydrogen combination \( \sigma_{HH}^{*} \) has the symmetry needed to mix with carbon \(2p_x\), while carbon \(2p_y\) is left with no partner of matching symmetry and therefore remains nonbonding.

visuals.label: Figure 1.16
visuals.caption: The orbital scheme for methylene. It separates the problem into a three-orbital block of matching symmetry, a two-orbital block, and one untouched nonbonding p orbital.

paragraphs.text:
The first step is purely organizational but it governs everything that follows.
With the bent \( \mathrm{H\!-\!C\!-\!H} \) geometry, the relevant symmetry elements are the molecular plane, the plane that reflects one hydrogen into the other, and the twofold rotation axis through carbon.
When the orbitals are classified with respect to those operations, \( \sigma_{HH} \), \(2s\), and \(2p_z\) fall into the same symmetry species, whereas \( \sigma_{HH}^{*} \) and \(2p_x\) form a second matched pair.
This is why the problem is not one bond interacting with one orbital, but rather a three-orbital mixing problem plus a separate two-orbital mixing problem.

visuals.label: Figure 1.16
visuals.caption: Symmetry labels for methylene. The key point is that only orbitals in the same symmetry class are allowed to mix.

paragraphs.text:
The general form of the three-orbital problem is the obvious extension of the two-orbital case:
\[
\psi = c_1 \phi_1 + c_2 \phi_2 + c_3 \phi_3 .
\]
That is Equation 1.8, and the coefficients are determined by solving the corresponding three-by-three secular problem.
If one thinks directly in a left-hydrogen, carbon, right-hydrogen basis, symmetry forces the two hydrogen contributions to have equal magnitude, while the carbon coefficient is free to differ.
Because the carbon \(2s\) orbital starts lower in energy than carbon \(2p_z\), the lowest orbital produced by this block is mostly \(2s\)-like, with only a smaller in-phase admixture of \(2p_z\).
That is why the lowest orbital in Figure 1.17 still looks largely \(s\)-like.

visuals.label: Figure 1.17
visuals.caption: How the hydrogen bonding combination mixes with carbon 2 s and 2 p z. The lowest orbital stays mostly s-like because the 2 s orbital starts lower in energy.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 8 writes the molecular orbital as a linear combination of three basis orbitals.
equations.note: It is the three-orbital analogue of the earlier two-orbital L C A O formula, and it leads to a three-by-three secular problem.

paragraphs.text:
The middle orbital in this three-orbital block is more revealing.
There the hydrogen combination and the carbon \(2p_z\) orbital are in phase, while the carbon \(2s\) contribution is out of phase, so the upper lobe is reinforced and the lower one is diminished.
The highest orbital in the block has both carbon orbitals out of phase with the hydrogen combination and is antibonding overall, even though the book does not draw it in the simpler schematic of Figure 1.16.
So the three matched orbitals produce three molecular orbitals, exactly as linear algebra says they must.

visuals.label: Figure 1.17
visuals.caption: The three orbitals created from the matched methylene block. One is largely s-like, one has strong p character in the molecular plane, and the highest is antibonding overall.

paragraphs.text:
The second symmetry block is easier.
The antibonding hydrogen combination \( \sigma_{HH}^{*} \) mixes with \(2p_x\) to give a bonding orbital \( \sigma_x \) and its antibonding partner, while \(2p_y\) stays essentially unchanged because no orbital of the same symmetry is available.
If the molecule were forced to be linear, the head-on overlap responsible for \( \sigma_x \) would improve, but the hydrogen atoms would then sit in the nodal plane of \(2p_z\), so the \( \sigma_z \) bonding would disappear.
The bent structure wins because it keeps both kinds of stabilization at once, and the remaining empty \(2p_y\) orbital becomes the lowest unfilled, nonbonding orbital.

visuals.label: Figure 1.16
visuals.caption: Why bent methylene is favored. Bending keeps both the sigma x and sigma z stabilizations, whereas a linear arrangement would lose one of them.

sections.title: Hybridisation as a change of picture

paragraphs.text:
The full molecular-orbital description is more faithful, but it has one practical inconvenience.
In methane or methylene there is no single delocalized orbital that can simply be pointed to and called the C–H bond.
Hybridisation is Pauling's remedy for that inconvenience.
It does not introduce new physics, but reorganizes the same \(s\) and \(p\) ingredients into orbitals already directed toward the bonds we wish to discuss.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: Hybrid orbitals built from s and p functions. The point is not new physics but a more bond-directed way of organizing the same atomic ingredients.

paragraphs.text:
The simplest case is \(sp\) hybridisation.
If we want two equivalent orbitals pointing in opposite directions, we add and subtract \(s\) and \(p_x\) with equal weight.
Normalization and orthogonality then force the standard pair
\[
h_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(s+p_x), \qquad h_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(s-p_x).
\]
The coefficients are not decoration: the square of each coefficient contributes to the total norm, so \(1/2+1/2=1\), and the opposite signs make the two hybrids orthogonal.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: The sp pair. Equal positive and negative combinations of s and p x create two opposite, equivalent hybrids.

paragraphs.text:
For \(sp^2\) hybridisation we want three equivalent orbitals in one plane, separated by \(120^\circ\).
That means we need three normalized linear combinations built from one \(s\) orbital and two \(p\) orbitals, with every pair orthogonal.
One convenient choice, matching the geometry of Figure 1.18, is
\[
h_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}s-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}p_z,\qquad
h_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}s+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}p_z,\qquad
h_3=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}s-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\,p_z .
\]
Each hybrid is normalized because the squares of its coefficients add to 1, and any two are orthogonal because their positive and negative cross terms cancel exactly.
The unused \(2p_y\) orbital then stands perpendicular to the plane and becomes the familiar orbital available for \(\pi\) bonding in trigonal carbon compounds.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: One convenient sp squared set. Three normalized combinations lie in one plane and point 120 degrees apart, while one p orbital remains unused.

paragraphs.text:
For \(sp^3\) hybridisation we want four equivalent directions aimed at the corners of a tetrahedron.
In the coordinate choice used in Figure 1.18, a convenient orthonormal set is
\[
h_1=\frac12 s-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_x-\frac12 p_z,\qquad
h_2=\frac12 s+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_x-\frac12 p_z,
\]
\[
h_3=\frac12 s-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_y+\frac12 p_z,\qquad
h_4=\frac12 s+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}p_y+\frac12 p_z.
\]
Again the logic is the same: each hybrid has norm \(1\), and the dot product of any two different hybrids is \(0\).
Figure 1.19 is valuable because it corrects the cartoon.
Since the \(2s\) orbital itself has an inner lobe, the hybrid does not consist of one clean front teardrop and one detached back bump; the nucleus actually sits inside the back lobe, and some of the front lobe wraps behind it.

visuals.label: Figure 1.18
visuals.caption: A tetrahedrally directed sp cubed set. The coefficients encode one convenient orthonormal basis for four bond-directed hybrids.

visuals.label: Figure 1.19
visuals.caption: A contour slice through an sp cubed hybrid. It corrects the usual cartoon by showing the back lobe wrapped around the nucleus rather than detached from it.

paragraphs.text:
Once the hybrid is built, the bond is treated exactly as before.
A hydrogen \(1s\) orbital and a carbon \(sp^3\) hybrid combine to give a bonding \( \sigma_{CH} \) orbital and an antibonding \( \sigma_{CH}^{*} \) orbital, as shown in Figure 1.20.
Four such bonding orbitals generate the familiar tetrahedral picture of methane in Figure 1.21.
This localized picture is often useful because it makes the equality of the four C–H bonds immediately obvious.
But the book is right to insist on the caveat: hybridisation is a picture we impose for convenience, not a literal event occurring inside the molecule.
So it is better to say that a carbon center is tetrahedral or trigonal than to imagine the atom itself mysteriously rehybridizing.

visuals.label: Figure 1.20
visuals.caption: A hydrogen 1 s orbital and a carbon sp cubed hybrid combine into one bonding and one antibonding C H orbital.

visuals.label: Figure 1.21
visuals.caption: Methane rebuilt from four sp cubed hybrids. This picture makes the equality of the four C H bonds immediately visible.

sections.title: Ethane and the major part of the C–C sigma bond

paragraphs.text:
Ethane has fourteen valence electrons, and the complete molecular-orbital diagram is already crowded.
Figure 1.22 therefore does something sensible: it shows the seven bonding orbitals and only three representative antibonding ones, then asks us to focus on the orbitals that actually hold the two carbon atoms together.
This is the first real C–C bond in the chapter, and it is important that the book does not reduce it too quickly to a single line on a structural formula.

visuals.label: Figure 1.22
visuals.caption: The crowded bonding picture of ethane. It is useful mainly because it lets us isolate which orbitals actually contribute to the C C bond.

paragraphs.text:
Two of the relevant orbitals, \( \sigma_s \) and \( \sigma_s' \), are built largely from the carbon \(2s\) orbitals.
Like the hydrogen \( \sigma \) and \( \sigma^{*} \) orbitals, they place electron density directly on the internuclear axis, so they are genuinely \(\sigma\)-type with respect to the C–C bond.
The lower one is strongly bonding, the upper one is partly antibonding as far as C–C is concerned, and the balance does not cancel perfectly.
A third orbital, \( \sigma_x \), built mainly from the two carbon \(2p_x\) orbitals with some \(2s\) admixture, gives the most important single contribution to the C–C bond.
It does so because it is strongly bonding between the carbons while its antibonding partner stays empty.

visuals.label: Figure 1.22
visuals.caption: The s-like and x-like contributions to the C C bond in ethane. The x orbital is the strongest single C C bonding component because its antibonding partner is empty.

paragraphs.text:
The chapter then makes a subtle but worthwhile point.
Even in ethane, the \(2p_y\) and \(2p_z\) orbitals on one carbon can overlap sideways with the corresponding orbitals on the other carbon, so there are \(\pi\)-type interactions present.
They are weak because sideways overlap enhances electron density between the nuclei without placing that density directly on the internuclear line.
So the electrostatic shielding and stabilizing overlap are both less effective than in a head-on \(\sigma\) interaction.
Figure 1.23 expresses that geometrically.
At a typical C–C single-bond distance, the \(\pi\)-overlap integral is only a modest fraction of the corresponding \(\sigma\)-overlap integral.
The bonding and antibonding \(\pi\)-type combinations in ethane therefore more or less cancel each other as far as the C–C bond is concerned.

visuals.label: Figure 1.23
visuals.caption: Comparison of sigma and pi overlap for carbon p orbitals. At single-bond distances the sideways pi overlap is much smaller than the head-on sigma overlap.

paragraphs.text:
That is why the book allows itself, in Figure 1.24, to localize a major part of the C–C bond into a single bonding orbital \( \sigma_x \) and a single antibonding orbital \( \sigma_x^{*} \).
This is not the whole truth, but it is an honest shorthand.
Had we switched to the hybrid language, we would have described the same main feature as overlap of two \(sp^3\) hybrids directed toward each other.
The two descriptions differ in bookkeeping, not in physics.

visuals.label: Figure 1.24
visuals.caption: A localized shorthand for the main sigma part of the C C bond. It is not the whole ethane wave function, but it captures the dominant bonding pair.

sections.title: Ethylene and the emergence of the pi bond

paragraphs.text:
Ethylene is the point at which the chapter's separation between \(\sigma\) and \(\pi\) ideas becomes decisive.
Ten of the twelve valence electrons occupy the lower-lying \(\sigma\)-framework orbitals built from the hydrogen \(1s\) orbitals and the carbon \(2s\), \(2p_x\), and \(2p_y\) orbitals.
The remaining pair occupies an orbital built entirely from the two carbon \(2p_z\) orbitals.
Because the hydrogens lie in the nodal plane of \(2p_z\), they do not mix into this orbital, and the \(\pi\) bond becomes a self-contained two-center problem sitting on top of the \(\sigma\) skeleton.

visuals.label: Figure 1.25
visuals.caption: The ethylene sigma framework together with the separate p z orbital pair. The hydrogens sit in the nodal plane of the p system, so the pi bond becomes an almost self-contained problem.

visuals.label: Figure 1.26
visuals.caption: The simple two-level interaction diagram for the C double bond pi system.

paragraphs.text:
For that isolated \(\pi\) problem, the linear combination reduces again to two terms.
If \( \phi_1 \) and \( \phi_2 \) are the two carbon \(p_z\) orbitals, then the normalized combinations are
\[
\psi_{\pi}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\phi_1+\phi_2), \qquad
\psi_{\pi^{*}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\phi_1-\phi_2).
\]
In simple Hückel theory one neglects overlap in the denominator, so the secular problem gives
\[
E_{\pi}=\alpha+\beta,\qquad E_{\pi^{*}}=\alpha-\beta .
\]
Since \( \beta \) is negative, the first combination is lowered and the second is raised.
The coefficients are \(0.707\) and \(0.707\) in the bonding orbital, and \(0.707\) and \(-0.707\) in the antibonding orbital.
That happens because the two atoms are equivalent and the normalized symmetric and antisymmetric combinations differ only by sign.
With two electrons in \( \psi_{\pi} \), the total \(\pi\) stabilization is \(2|\beta|\), which the book estimates for ethylene as about \(280\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}\).

visuals.label: Figure 1.26
visuals.caption: Bonding and antibonding combinations of the two p z orbitals on carbon. The sign pattern alone determines whether electron density is built between the nuclei or split by a node.

paragraphs.text:
The comparison with the particle in a box is not a trick but a structural analogy.
For a one-dimensional box of length \(L\), the stationary waves are
\[
\psi_n(x)=\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right), \qquad n=1,2,\dots
\]
and the endpoints are nodes.
The lowest mode has no internal node and corresponds to the bonding \(\pi\) orbital, while the next mode has one internal node and corresponds to \( \pi^{*} \).
When the sine curve is sampled at the two carbon positions, the first mode gives coefficients of the same sign on both atoms and the second gives coefficients of opposite sign.
That is exactly the pattern shown in Figure 1.27.

visuals.label: Figure 1.27
visuals.caption: The particle in a box analogy for ethylene. The lowest sine mode matches the bonding sign pattern, and the next mode matches the antibonding one.

paragraphs.text:
Figures 1.28 and 1.29 then repair the usual schematic drawing.
The contour plot shows that the bonding \(\pi\) orbital does indeed increase electron density between the carbons, but the density sits above and below the bond axis instead of directly on it.
That is why a \(\pi\) bond strengthens a C=C bond without ever becoming as effective per unit overlap as a head-on \(\sigma\) bond.
The wire-mesh drawings make the same point three-dimensionally.
They are a fitting close to this batch because they show the chapter's central lesson in one glance: bonding is controlled not by lines in a formula, but by the geometry, symmetry, and phase of the underlying wave functions.

visuals.label: Figure 1.28
visuals.caption: Contour sections of the pi and pi star orbitals of ethylene. They show directly where the electron density is enhanced and where the node falls.

visuals.label: Figure 1.29
visuals.caption: Wire-mesh views of one contour of the pi and pi star orbitals. The three-dimensional shape makes the above-and-below character of pi bonding immediately visible.

paragraphs.text: Conjugation begins with the same basic act that created the \(\pi\) bond in ethylene: neighbouring \(p\) orbitals are allowed to overlap and to spread one electronic system over more than one atom. The next step is simply to keep adding one \(p\) orbital at a time, so that the two-centre \(\pi\) bond of ethylene grows into the three-centre allyl system, then into butadiene, pentadienyl, and longer chains. Hückel theory becomes possible because we make one decisive simplification: we separate the \(\sigma\) framework from the \(\pi\) system, and then treat the \(\pi\) electrons as though they live in their own self-contained problem. That separation is what allows the electron-in-a-box analogy to keep working, and it is why conjugated \(\pi\) systems can be discussed with relatively simple wave patterns instead of the full many-orbital complexity of the whole molecule.

The allyl family contains three closely related species: the allyl cation, the allyl radical, and the allyl anion. They share the same \(\sigma\) skeleton and the same three \(p\) orbitals, but they do not have the same number of \(\pi\) electrons. The cation has two \(\pi\) electrons, the radical has three, and the anion has four, so the orbitals are the same while the occupancy changes.

visuals.caption: A localized sketch of the allyl cation, useful as a practical shorthand but not a literal picture of a fixed charge locked on one carbon.
A localized sketch of the allyl radical, showing the odd electron on one end in the conventional shorthand.
A localized sketch of the allyl anion, again useful as shorthand before the fuller delocalized picture is restored.

paragraphs.text: The familiar localised drawings are useful, but they must be handled with care. A drawing that places the positive charge, the odd electron, or the negative charge on one end of the allyl system does not mean that the species literally exists as one static local structure and then flips into another. The paired drawings joined by a double-headed arrow are two representations of the same species, not two minima connected by a reaction step. For that reason, the double-headed arrow must mark resonance only, and it must not be used as though it meant equilibrium. Those localised drawings remain valuable because they show something real, namely that both the total \(\pi\) density and the most important frontier orbital place a great deal of weight on the two terminal carbons. If one wants a less misleading visual summary, one may draw the allyl cation, radical, or anion with a dashed delocalised \(\pi\) bond and the charge or odd electron placed in the middle. Even that cleaner picture has a cost, because it becomes awkward in mechanism drawing and it hides the fact that the terminal carbons are where much of the important frontier character actually sits.

visuals.label: 1.4a
visuals.caption: One resonance drawing of the allyl cation, emphasizing one terminal carbon.

visuals.label: 1.4b
visuals.caption: The partner resonance drawing of the allyl cation, reminding the reader that the species is one delocalized object, not two reacting structures.

visuals.label: 1.4c
visuals.caption: A delocalized allyl-cation sketch that suppresses the false impression of a fixed localized charge.

visuals.label: 1.5a
visuals.caption: One localized resonance sketch of the allyl radical.

visuals.label: 1.5b
visuals.caption: The companion resonance sketch of the allyl radical, again showing that the odd electron has strong terminal character.

visuals.label: 1.5c
visuals.caption: A delocalized allyl-radical sketch that highlights orbital spreading but hides some mechanistic convenience.

visuals.label: 1.6a
visuals.caption: One localized resonance sketch of the allyl anion.

visuals.label: 1.6b
visuals.caption: The companion allyl-anion sketch, useful because it makes the terminal concentration of excess electron density easy to imagine.

visuals.label: 1.6c
visuals.caption: A delocalized allyl-anion sketch that makes the spreading of the pi system explicit.

paragraphs.text:
Beneath those drawings lies the same bent \(\sigma\) framework for all three species, although for the sake of the \(\pi\)-system discussion it is standard to idealise the allyl unit as linear. That simplification changes the local coordinate bookkeeping, but it does not change the qualitative story. Once the \(\sigma\) framework is set aside, the \(\pi\) problem is built from the three unused \(p_z\) orbitals on the three carbons. So we begin with the three-term linear combination
\[
\psi = c_1 p_1 + c_2 p_2 + c_3 p_3 .
\]
This is Equation 1.9 in substance, and it simply says that any allyl \(\pi\) molecular orbital is being sought as a mixture of the three carbon \(p\) orbitals.

visuals.caption: The common sigma framework of the allyl family, shown apart from the pi system so that the pi problem can be treated on its own.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 9 writes an allyl pi molecular orbital as a linear combination of the three carbon p orbitals.
equations.note: This is the starting point for building the three allyl pi orbitals.

paragraphs.text: Because there are three basis orbitals, we must obtain three molecular orbitals. The electron-in-a-box picture tells us how to organise them. The lowest orbital should have no internal node, the next should have one internal node, and the highest should have two internal nodes, with the usual extra nodes at the ends of the box. So the three allyl orbitals correspond to the first three standing-wave patterns that fit a three-centre chain. In the lowest orbital, \(\psi_1\), all three coefficients have the same sign, so the wave function is bonding across the whole chain. In the middle orbital, \(\psi_2\), symmetry forces the node to sit on the central carbon, and that single fact has an immediate algebraic consequence: the coefficient on the middle atom must be zero, so \(c_2 = 0\) in \(\psi_2\).

visuals.label: Figure 1.30
visuals.caption: The three allyl pi orbitals shown as standing-wave patterns, with their signs and relative amplitudes laid out across the three carbons.

paragraphs.text:
Normalization then gives the other two coefficients. Since the total probability for one electron in one orbital must be one, we need
\[
c_1^2 + c_3^2 = 1 .
\]
By symmetry the two terminal carbons must contribute equally, so \(c_1 = -c_3\) in sign and \(|c_1| = |c_3|\) in magnitude. Hence
\[
2c_1^2 = 1,
\qquad
c_1^2 = \frac{1}{2},
\qquad
|c_1| = |c_3| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} .
\]
That is why the nonbonding orbital has coefficients \(+1/\sqrt{2}, 0, -1/\sqrt{2}\), up to an overall change of sign.

visuals.label: Figure 1.30
visuals.caption: The coefficient pattern of the allyl nonbonding orbital, showing equal terminal weight and zero weight on the central atom.

paragraphs.text:
The coefficient pattern in the other two orbitals is then fixed by orthogonality and normalization. Because \(\psi_1\), \(\psi_2\), and \(\psi_3^\ast\) must be mutually orthogonal and each individually normalized, the remaining coefficients are forced into the familiar set
\[
\psi_1 : \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}\right),
\qquad
\psi_2 : \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right),
\qquad
\psi_3^\ast : \left(\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}\right) .
\]
So the middle orbital is nonbonding not by verbal decree, but because its node sits exactly on the central atom and leaves only two terminal lobes that are too far apart to interact strongly in the first approximation.

visuals.label: Figure 1.30
visuals.caption: The full allyl coefficient set, showing the low bonding orbital, the middle nonbonding orbital, and the upper antibonding orbital.

paragraphs.text:
The book then gives a geometrical rule for the energies. Written relative to the \(\alpha\) level, the Hückel energies for a linear chain of \(n\) atoms are
\[
\Delta E_j = 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{j\pi}{n+1}\right),
\]
where \(j = 1,2,\dots,n\) labels the orbital. If one restores the reference level explicitly, the same statement is
\[
E_j = \alpha + 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{j\pi}{n+1}\right) .
\]
This is the form that underlies Equation 1.10.

visuals.label: Figure 1.31
visuals.caption: The circle construction that places the allyl and ethylene pi levels relative to the alpha reference line and shows the symmetric splitting above and below it.

equations.spoken: Equation 1 point 10 gives the Hückel energy levels relative to alpha for a linear conjugated chain.
equations.note: The cosine factor comes from the standing-wave pattern allowed on a chain of n atoms.

paragraphs.text:
The derivation is simple once one uses the standing-wave picture. In a box with \(n\) atomic sites and one extra node beyond each end, the allowed coefficient patterns are sine waves sampled at equally spaced positions. So the coefficient on atom \(k\) in orbital \(j\) is proportional to
\[
c_k^{(j)} \propto \sin\!\left(\frac{jk\pi}{n+1}\right) .
\]
When those sine patterns are inserted into the nearest-neighbour Hückel equations, the discrete second-difference created by coupling to neighbouring atoms produces the cosine factor, and the eigenvalue becomes \(2\beta \cos(j\pi/(n+1))\) relative to \(\alpha\). For allyl we have \(n=3\), so the three relative energies are
\[
\Delta E_1 = 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \sqrt{2}\,\beta,
\qquad
\Delta E_2 = 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{4}\right) = 0,
\qquad
\Delta E_3 = 2\beta \cos\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{4}\right) = -\sqrt{2}\,\beta .
\]
Since this book uses \(\beta\) largely as a positive magnitude on its diagrams, it is clearer to say that one allyl orbital lies \(1.414\beta\) below \(\alpha\), one sits at \(\alpha\), and one lies \(1.414\beta\) above \(\alpha\). That is exactly the pattern drawn in Figure 1.31.

visuals.label: Figure 1.31
visuals.caption: The allyl energy pattern evaluated explicitly, with one orbital below alpha, one on alpha, and one above alpha.

paragraphs.text: There is also a second and very instructive way to build the same picture. Instead of mixing three isolated \(p\) orbitals at once, begin from ethylene, which already has a bonding \(\pi\) orbital and an antibonding \(\pi^\ast\) orbital, and then let a third \(p\) orbital approach them. If the incoming \(p\) orbital interacts with the ethylene \(\pi\) orbital in phase, it creates a still more bonding orbital, which is \(\psi_1\). If that same incoming \(p\) orbital interacts with the ethylene \(\pi^\ast\) orbital out of phase, it creates the highest antibonding orbital, which is \(\psi_3^\ast\). The remaining orbital cannot be missing, because three starting orbitals must still give three final orbitals. It is the combination that is antibonding with respect to \(\pi\) but bonding with respect to \(\pi^\ast\), and those two tendencies balance to give the nonbonding orbital \(\psi_2\). So Figures 1.32 and 1.33 are not giving a different theory. They are rebuilding the same allyl orbitals from a different starting decomposition, and they make the origin of the nonbonding level especially easy to see.

visuals.label: Figure 1.32
visuals.caption: A third p orbital interacting separately with the ethylene pi and pi star orbitals, a useful intermediate picture before the final allyl set is recombined.

visuals.label: Figure 1.33
visuals.caption: The recombined allyl picture, where the balanced middle orbital appears naturally as the nonbonding level.

paragraphs.text: Once the orbitals are known, the total \(\pi\) electron populations follow from squaring the coefficients and weighting by occupancy. For the allyl cation, only \(\psi_1\) is occupied. Its squared coefficients are \(1/4\), \(1/2\), and \(1/4\), so with two electrons the \(\pi\) population is \(0.5\) on C-1, \(1.0\) on C-2, and \(0.5\) on C-3. Since each carbon nucleus contributes a charge of \(+1\) in this bookkeeping, the electron deficiency is concentrated at the two ends, giving an effective positive charge of about \(+0.5\) on each terminal carbon.

visuals.label: Figure 1.34
visuals.caption: Wave functions and one-electron populations for the allyl orbitals, making it easy to square coefficients and count where the pi electrons sit.

visuals.label: Figure 1.35
visuals.caption: The total pi electron population of the allyl cation, showing that the electron deficiency is concentrated at the two ends.

paragraphs.text: For the allyl anion, both \(\psi_1\) and \(\psi_2\) are filled. Adding the populations from the two occupied orbitals gives \(1.5\) electrons on C-1, \(1.0\) on C-2, and \(1.5\) on C-3. So the excess negative charge also sits mainly on the two terminal carbons, again about \(-0.5\) on each end in this simple picture. The allyl radical lies between those two cases. Its singly occupied molecular orbital is \(\psi_2\), so the odd electron is concentrated on the terminal carbons and absent from the central carbon in the idealized nonbonding picture.

visuals.label: Figure 1.35
visuals.caption: The total pi electron population of the allyl anion, showing that the excess electron density is concentrated on the two terminal carbons.

paragraphs.text: This is why the localised drawings are more useful than they first appear. They do not literally describe isolated fixed structures, but they do capture the most important charge pattern for the cation and anion, and the most important singly occupied pattern for the radical. They also anticipate the frontier-orbital story. For the allyl cation, the highest occupied molecular orbital is \(\psi_1\) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital is \(\psi_2\). For the allyl anion, the highest occupied molecular orbital is \(\psi_2\) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital is \(\psi_3^\ast\). For the allyl radical, the key frontier orbital is the singly occupied molecular orbital, and that is again \(\psi_2\). So the same orbital that looked nonbonding in the energy picture becomes the decisive reactive orbital for the radical, and one of the decisive frontier orbitals for the cation and anion as well.

visuals.label: 1.4a
visuals.caption: A resonance sketch of the allyl cation that also doubles as a practical guide to where the low-lying empty frontier orbital is concentrated.

visuals.label: 1.6a
visuals.caption: A resonance sketch of the allyl anion that usefully mirrors the HOMO distribution at the two ends of the system.

visuals.label: 1.5a
visuals.caption: A resonance sketch of the allyl radical that emphasizes the terminal weight of the singly occupied orbital.

paragraphs.text: There is one final caveat. The middle orbital is called nonbonding because, in the first approximation, the two terminal lobes do not interact through the central atom. But the more realistic three-dimensional pictures show that the interaction is not exactly zero. The terminal lobes are not infinitely far apart, the geometry is not perfectly linear, and the actual orbital is slightly distorted, so \(\psi_2\) sits a little above the energy of a truly isolated \(p\) orbital rather than exactly on it.

visuals.label: Figure 1.36
visuals.caption: A more realistic three-dimensional view of the allyl orbitals, showing that the middle level is only approximately nonbonding because the terminal lobes still weakly sense each other.
